name: Papal Infallibility
description: Papal Infallibility, in Catholic theology, is the dogma that the Pope, when speaking ex cathedra (i.e., when officially declaring a doctrine relating to faith or morals to be definitively held by the entire Church), is preserved from the possibility of error. It is not a claim that the Pope is impeccable or cannot sin, nor does it apply to his private opinions or non-definitive teachings. The doctrine was formally defined at the First Vatican Council in 1870, though its roots can be traced back to early Church history and the Petrine primacy (Matthew 16:18-19). Proponents argue that infallibility is necessary to ensure the Church's ability to teach authentic doctrine and maintain unity, citing biblical passages and the constant tradition of the Church. Critics, both within and outside the Catholic Church, question the historical basis for the doctrine, pointing to instances where papal pronouncements have appeared to contradict each other or earlier teachings. Some scholars argue that the definition of infallibility was influenced by political and social factors during the 19th century. Mainstream Catholic theology emphasizes that infallibility is limited in scope and application, and that the Pope is always subject to divine revelation and the teachings of Scripture and Tradition. It is seen as a charism granted to the Church as a whole, with the Pope serving as the instrument through which this charism is exercised in specific circumstances. The Second Vatican Council (1962-1965) further clarified the understanding of infallibility by emphasizing the role of the College of Bishops in teaching authority.
subtitle: Dogma of papal teaching authority.

claims.claim: The Pope is infallible when speaking ex cathedra on matters of faith and morals.
claims.evidence: Matthew 16:18-19 (the Petrine Primacy); Luke 22:32 (Christ's prayer for Peter's faith); John 21:15-17 (Jesus entrusting Peter to feed His sheep); historical precedent of papal pronouncements settling doctrinal disputes.
claims.criticism: Biblical interpretations are contested. Historical examples of papal pronouncements appearing contradictory or erroneous exist. The concept of 'ex cathedra' is open to interpretation, leading to ambiguity.

claims.claim: Papal Infallibility is necessary to preserve the unity and truth of the Church.
claims.evidence: Without a final, authoritative voice, doctrinal disputes would be irresolvable, leading to fragmentation and heresy. The Church has consistently maintained a central teaching authority.
claims.criticism: Other Christian denominations maintain unity without a single infallible figure. The historical record shows instances of internal dissent and disagreement within the Catholic Church despite the claim of infallibility.

claims.claim: Papal infallibility is a charism given to the Church as a whole, exercised through the Pope.
claims.evidence: The Church's constant teaching authority and witness to the faith throughout history. The Pope acts as the instrument through which the Holy Spirit guides the Church.
claims.criticism: This interpretation is a later development, formulated to address concerns about potential abuses of papal power. It does not adequately explain instances where papal pronouncements have been challenged or revised.

keyFigures.name: Pope Pius IX
keyFigures.role: Reigned during the First Vatican Council, which formally defined Papal Infallibility.

keyFigures.name: Hans Küng
keyFigures.role: Catholic theologian who strongly criticized the doctrine of Papal Infallibility.

mythologyConnections: The concept of a divinely appointed leader with special knowledge or authority
Analogies to oracles or prophets in other religions

timeline.event: First Vatican Council formally defines Papal Infallibility.
Papal pronouncements on matters of faith and morals throughout Church history.
Debate and discussion about the interpretation and application of Papal Infallibility.
